title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 20 — Cao Cao Organises a Hunting Expedition / Dong Cheng Receives a Secret Decree in the Palace
text:
Just asCao Caohad raised his sword and was about to slayZhang Liao, it wasXuandewho grabbed him by the arm andYunchangwho knelt before him.

“Men who bare their hearts as he does ought to be retained,” saidXuande.

AndYunchangadded, “I have always knownWenyuanto be a loyal and righteous fellow. I will vouch for him with my own life.”

Cao Caothrew aside his sword and smiled. “I am well aware ofWenyuan’s loyalty and righteousness; I was only teasing him.”

And he removedZhang Liao’s bonds, took off his own robe and placed it onZhang Liao, and led him to the seat of honor. Moved by these shows of affection,Zhang Liaoyielded, andCao Caoappointed him as General of the Household Gentlemen and as a Marquis Within The Passes.

Cao CaosentZhang Liaoto convinceZang Bato submit as well, and whenZang Balearned thatLü Buwas already dead and thatZhang Liaohad yielded, he too led his own forces to offer his surrender;Cao Caorichly rewarded him.Zang Bain turn convincedSun Guan,Wu Dun, andYin Lito come and surrender as well; onlyChang Xiremained unwilling to give in.Cao CaoappointedZang Baas Chancellor to the Prince of Langye, whileSun Guanand the others too were each granted offices and charged with protecting the parts of Qingzhou and Xuzhou along the coast.Lü Bu’s wife and daughter were transferred to the capital at Xudu.

After the soldiers had been rewarded with feastings, the camp was broken up and the army marched back for the capital. As they passed through Xuzhou, the people lined the roads and burned incense in honor of the victors, and they asked that Commissioner Liu be left in the region to serve as their governor.

Cao Caotold them, “Commissioner Liu has rendered great services; first let him be received in audience by His Majesty and granted his noble title, then there will be time enough for him to return.” The people bowed low to the ground to express their thanks. In the meantime,Cao Caoassigned the General of Chariots and Cavalry,Che Zhou, to manage affairs in Xuzhou.

OnceCao Cao’s army had returned to the capital, he granted titles and rewards to those who had accompanied the expedition, and he lodgedXuandein an annex joined to his own ministerial office. The following day asEmperor Xianheld court,Cao Caosubmitted a petition extollingXuande’s military achievements, and when he hadXuandebrought in for the audience,Xuandeappeared in court attire and performed obeisance at the foot of the stairs leading into the hall.Emperor Xiansummoned him up into the hall and asked him, “What is your ancestry?”

Xuandereplied, “Thy subject is a descendant ofPrince Jing of Zhongshan, thus I am of the lineage of the Filial Emperor Jing; I am the grandson ofLiu Xiongand the son ofLiu Hong.”

The Emperorordered his ministers to consult the Books of the Genealogies, and the Minister of the Imperial Clan read out:

“The Filial Emperor Jing sired fourteen sons, of whomPrince Jing of Zhongshan,Liu Sheng, was the seventh.

Liu Shengsired the Marquis of Lucheng District,Liu Zhen.

Liu Zhensired the Marquis of Pei, Liu Ang.

Liu Ang sired the Marquis of Zhang, Liu Lu.

Liu Lu sired the Marquis of Yishui, Liu Lian.

Liu Lian sired the Marquis of Qinyang, Liu Ying.

Liu Ying sired the Marquis of Anguo, Liu Jian.

Liu Jian sired the Marquis of Guangling, Liu Ai.

Liu Ai sired the Marquis of Jiaoshui, Liu Xia.

Liu Xia sired the Marquis of Zuyi, Liu Shu.

Liu Shu sired the Marquis of Qiyang, Liu Yi.

Liu Yi sired the Marquis of Yuanze, Liu Bi.

Liu Bi sired the Marquis of Yingchuan, Liu Da.

Liu Da sired the Marquis of Fengling, Liu Buyi.

Liu Buyi sired the Marquis of Jichuan, Liu Hui.

Liu Hui sired the Prefect of Fan county in Dong commandery,Liu Xiong.

Liu XiongsiredLiu Hong, who held no office.

AndLiu Beiis the son ofLiu Hong.”

Reckoning back his own lineage from the genealogies,Emperor Xianfound thatXuandewas thus a generation senior to him in kinship; delighted by the prospect, he invitedXuandeinto a side chamber where he could show him the sort of familial respect due from a nephew to an uncle. And he thought to himself, “Cao Caoabuses his power and none of the affairs of state derive from my own will, yet now that I have got this heroic uncle I shall have assistance!” Thus he appointedXuandeas General of the Left and Marquis of Yicheng District.

When the banquet was concluded,Xuandethankedthe Emperorfor his grace and went out of the court. And from that time on, people took to calling himImperial UncleLiu.

WhenCao Caoreturned to his office,Xun Yuand his fellow advisers went in to see him, telling him, “Illustrious Sir, we fear no good shall come to you from the Son of Heaven confirmingLiu Beias an uncle.”

“If he has been confirmed as an uncle,”Cao Caoreplied, “since I can wield the edicts of the Son of Heaven to command him, he will be even less likely to dare refuse than before. Not to mention that so long as I detain him at Xudu, I can claim that I want to have him near the sovereign while really keeping him in the palm of my hand; what do I have to fear? The one who actually worries me is the Grand Commandant,Yang Biao; as he is bound toYuan Shuby kinship, if he were to conspire with the two Yuans from within the capital, he could cause untold damage. I ought to get rid of him at once.”

HenceCao Caosecretly had someone slanderYang Biao, claiming that he was in communication withYuan Shu, and on this charge he arrestedYang Biaoand imprisoned him, orderingMan Chongto prosecute him.

By this time, the Administrator ofBohai,Kong Rong, was atthe capitalas well. He remonstrated withCao Cao, saying, “Yang Biaocomes from a family renowned for their honesty and virtue for four generations; how can you accuse him on account of the Yuan clan?”

“But this is a court matter,”Cao Caoprotested.

“And I suppose ifKing Cheng of Zhouhad wanted to put the Duke of Shao to death, his brother the Duke of Zhou would likewise have just thrown up his hands as well?” saidKong Rong.

Thus feeling compelled to act,Cao Caoonly hadYang Biaoremoved from office and sent home to his estate.

Moved by indignation atCao Cao’s domination of the court, one of the Gentlemen-Councilors,Zhao Yan, sent up a petition censuringCao Caofor the crime of presuming to arrest a chief minister without the authorization of an imperial decree. Infuriated,Cao CaohadZhao Yanarrested and killed; not one of the officials failed to quiver in fear.

The adviserCheng YupersuadedCao Cao, “Illustrious Sir, your prestige grows daily; why not seize the opportunity to become king or hegemon in your own right?”

“There are still too many supporters of the court,” was his reply; “I cannot act rashly. I shall invite the Son of Heaven on a hunt in order to observe how people react.”

And he selected and picked out fleet horses, famous falcons, and pedigree hounds and prepared bows and arrows in readiness.

OnceCao Caohad mustered his soldiers outside the city, he went into the palace to invite the Son of Heaven on the hunt.Emperor Xiantold him, “I fear it is improper to go hunting.”

Cao Caoreplied, “Even the sovereigns of antiquity had their ritual hunts in spring and summer, autumn and winter, going out into the environs of the capital each season in order to display their martial valor to the realm. And as the land within the Four Seas is wracked by turmoil, it would be proper to borrow the occasion of a hunt in order to provide martial vigor.” The Emperor did not dare to refuse.

Sothe Emperorwith the full paraphernalia for an Imperial hunt joined the expedition: he rode a saddled horse and carried an inlaid bow with a quiver of gold-tipped arrows, while the imperial procession followed behind.Xuandeand his brothers were among them, each bringing a bow and quiver, wearing a breastplate under their robe, and wielding their weapon as they led several dozen riders to accompany the imperial carriage out of the capital.

Cao Caorode a dun horse called “Flying-Lightning”, leading an army of a hundred thousand as they escorted the Son of Heaven to go hunting at Xutian; the soldiers spread out to form a great enclosure, more than two hundred li wide.

Cao Caorode even withthe Emperor, their horses’ heads alternating in the lead, while his own confidantes all kept close behind them. The other civil and military officials only followed at a distance, none daring to draw any nearer.

AsEmperor Xianwas galloping his horse towards Xutian,Liu Xuandesaluted him while standing at the roadside.

“I should like to seemy uncledisplay his hunting skill,” saidthe Emperor.

Xuandemounted his steed at once. Just then from out of the bushes leaped a hare:Xuandeshot and hit it with the first arrow.

The Emperor, much struck by this display, rode away over a slope. Suddenly a deer broke out of the thicket. He shot three arrows at it but all missed.

He turned toCao Caoand said, “You shoot it.”

Cao Caohelped himself to the Son of Heaven’s inlaid bow and gold-tipped arrows, and nocking an arrow, he struck the deer in the back with a single arrow and sent it tumbling into the grass.

Now the crowd of officials, seeing the gold-tipped arrow sticking in the wound, concluded at once that the shot was the Son of Heaven’s, and they scrambled towardsEmperor Xianwhile exclaiming “Long live!” ButCao Caorode out, pushing pastthe Emperor, and acknowledged the congratulations instead. All of them turnedpale.1•••

The term [I]wansui[/I] (萬歲), literally “ten-thousand years,” may be translated as “long life!” the concept of a long life, or even immortality for a person.↩

BehindXuandewasYunchangin full fury; his sleeping caterpillar eyebrows mounted and his red phoenix eyes boggled, and he hefted his blade and beat his horse forward, intending to cutCao Caodown. Yet whenXuandespotted him he hastily waved him back with a look, and seeing his elder brother’s reaction,Lord Guandared move no further.

ThenXuandebowed towardCao Caoto congratulate him: “A divine shot, Prime Minister, such as few have achieved!”

“Just a bit of the Son of Heaven’s blessings rubbing off on me,” repliedCao Caowith a smile.

Then he turned his steed towards the Son of Heaven and congratulated him as well, though instead of offering back the inlaid bow he slung it over his own shoulder.

The hunt finished with banqueting, and when the entertainments were over they returned to the capital, all glad of some repose after the expedition.

YunchangaskedXuande, “Brother, why did you prevent me from killing that rebelCao Caoand so ridding the state of a threat? He cheats his master and insults his sovereign.”

“It’s like the old saying: ‘wanting to hit the rat, yet hesitating lest you break the vase’,”Xuandetold him. “Cao Caowas only a horse’s head away from the Emperor while his minions were crowded all around them. My brother, if you really had lashed out during that burst of anger, supposing you had missed and harmed the Son of Heaven instead, it would have been us charged with the treason.”

[Liu Bei is likely quoting the Han scholar Jia Yi.]

“If we do not kill this rebel today, he is sure to cause disaster,” saidYunchang.

“Yet be discreet, my brother. Such matters cannot be lightly discussed.”

In the meantime,Emperor Xianreturned to his palace, where he tearfully related what had occurred during the hunt to Empress Fu.

“Ever since I came to the throne, it’s been one conniving brute after another. First there were the machinations ofDong Zhuo, and afterwards the turbulence ofLi JueandGuo Si; not even the common man has endured the toils which you and I have known. Then when we gotCao Cao, I thought he would be the sort of minister who upholds the very altars of the state, never suspecting that he would dominate the state and abuse his authority, presuming to grant wealth and power to whomever he pleased; every time I meet with him, I feel ‘the thorn at my back’. And then today at the hunt, he accepted the congratulations for himself; how much worse can his disrespect get? He is sure to carry out some sinister scheme sooner or later, and then who knows what sort of deaths we shall suffer?”

["The thorn at my back" is probably a reference to the unease Emperor Xuan of Han felt whenever he met with his regent Huo Guang, especially during a particular ride together to the ancestral temple.]

“In a whole court full of ministers who have eaten the bread ofHan, is there not one who will save the state?” said she.

The words were hardly out of her mouth when suddenly entered a man to declare, “Worry not, Your Majesties! I have just the man who can remove this threat to the state.”

Emperor Xianturned to look, and saw it was Empress Fu’s fatherFu Wan.

“Have you too heard ofCao Cao’s wanton and perverse behavior?” said the Emperor, drying his eyes.

“You mean the deer shooting? Who did not see that indeed? But the whole court is full of his clan or his creatures. With the exception of the relatives of your Consort there is not one loyal enough to deal with a rebel. I have no authority to do anything, but there is GeneralDong Cheng, theState Uncle, who could do it.”

“CouldUncleDongcome in to consult about this? I know he has had much experience of State troubles.”

Fu Wanreplied, “Every one of your attendants is a partizan ofCao Cao’s and this sort of thing must be kept most profoundly secret or the consequence will be most serious.”

“Then what can be done?” saidthe Emperor.

“The only plan I can think of is to send gifts of a robe and a jade girdle toDong Cheng, and in the lining of the girdle hide a secret edict authorizing him to take certain steps. When he gets home and has read the edict he can elaborate plans as quickly as possible and neither the spirits above nor the demons below will know anything about them.”

The Emperorapproved andFu Wanwent out. The emperor then with his own hand drew up a decree, writing it with blood drawn by biting his finger. He gave the document to his consort to sew into the purple lining of the girdle. When all was done he put on the robe and girded it with the girdle. Next he bade one of the attendants summon Uncle Dong to the palace.

Dong Chengcame and after the ceremonies were finishedthe Empresssaid, “A few nights ago I was talking withthe Empressof the terrible days of the rebellion and we thought of your good services then, therefore we have called you in to reward you.”

The minister bowed his head in thanks. Thenthe EmperorledDong Chengout of the reception hall to theTemple of Ancestors, and they went to thePavilion of Worthy Ministers, where the Emperor burned incense and performed the usual ceremonies. After this they went to see the portraits, among which was one of the founder of theHan dynasty, Liu Bang, theSupreme Progenitor.

“Whence sprang ourgreat ancestorand how did he begin his great achievement?” saidthe Emperor.

“Your Excellencyis pleased to joke with thy servant,” saidDong Cheng, rather startled at the question. “Who does not know the deeds of theSacred Ancestor? He began life as a minor official inSishang. There gripping his sword he slew theWhite Serpent, the beginning of his struggle for the right. Speedily he mastered the Empire; in three years had destroyedQinand, in five, alsoChu. Thus he set upa dynastythat shall endure for ever.”

“Such heroic forefathers! such weakling descendants! How sad it is!” saidthe Emperor.

Pointing to the portraits right and left he continued, “Are not these twoZhang Liang,Marquisof Liu, andXiao He,Marquisof Cuo?”

“Certainly. The Supreme Ancestor was greatly assisted by these two.”

The Emperorglanced right and left. His attendants were rather far away. Then he whispered toDong Cheng, “You, like these two, must stand by me.”

“My poor services are of no worth; I do not compare with those men,” said theUncleDong.

“I remember that you saved me at the western capital. I have never forgotten and I could never reward you.” Then pointing to his own robethe Emperorcontinued, “You must wear this robe of mine, girded with my own girdle, and it will be as though you are always near your Emperor.”

Dong Chengbowed his gratitude while the Emperor, taking off the robe, presented it to his faithful Minister. At the same time he whispered, “Examine it closely when you get home, and help your Emperor carry out his intention.”

Dong Chengunderstood. He put on the robe and the girdle, took leave and left the chamber.

The news of the audience forDong Chenghad been taken to theAssisting Chancellor, who at once went to the Palace and arrived asDong Chengwas passing out at the gate. They met face to face andDong Chengcould in nowise avoid him. He went to the side of the road and made his obeisance.

“Where have you come from,State Uncle?” askedCao Cao.

“His Excellencysummoned me into the Palace and has given me this robe and beautiful girdle,” explainedDong Cheng.

“Why did he give you these?”

“He had not forgotten that I saved his life in the old days.”

“Take it off and let me see it.”

Dong Chengwho knew that a secret decree was hidden away somewhere in the garments was afraidCao Caowould notice a breach somewhere in the material, so he hesitated and did not obey. But the tyrant called his servants. So he took off the girdle.

Cao Caothen looked it over carefully. “It certainly is a very handsome girdle,” said he. “Now take off the robe and let me look at that.”

Dong Cheng’s heart was melting with fear but he dared not disobey. So he handed over the robe.

Cao Caotook it and held it up against the sun with his own hand and minutely examined every part of it. When he had done this he put it on, girded it with the girdle and turning to his suite said, “How is it for length?”

“Beautiful!” they chorussed.

Turning toDong Chenghe said, “Will you give these to me?”

“My King’s presents to me I dare not give to another. Let me give you another robe in its stead,” saidDong Cheng.

“Is there not some intrigue connected with these presents? I am sure there is,” saidCao Cao.

“How could I dare?” saidDong Cheng, trembling. “If you are so set upon it then I must give it up.”

“How could I take away what your King has given you? It was all a joke,” saidCao Cao.

He returned both robe and girdle and their owner made the best of his way home. When night came and he was alone in his library, he took out the robe and looked over every inch of it most carefully. He found nothing.

“He gave me a robe and a girdle and bade me look at them carefully. That means there is something to be looked for but I can find no trace of it. What does it mean?” he soliloquized.

Then he lifted the girdle and examined that. The jade plates were carved into the semblance of small dragons interlaced among flowers. The lining was of purple silk. All was sewn together most carefully and neatly and he could find nothing out of the common. He was puzzled. He laid the belt on the table. Presently he picked it up and looked at it again. He spent long hours over it but in vain. He leaned over on the small table, his head resting on his hands and was almost asleep, when a candle snuff fell down upon the girdle and burned a hole in the lining. He hastily shook it off, but the mischief was done: a small hole had been burned in the silken lining, and through this there appeared something white with blood red marks. He hastily ripped it open and drew out the decree written by the hand ofthe Emperorhimself in blood. It read:

Of human relationships, that between father and son stands first; of the various social ties that between Prince and Minister stands highest. TodayCao Cao, the wicked is a real tyrant, treating even his Prince with indignity. With the support of his faction and his army he has destroyed the principles of government. By conferring rewards and inflicting punishments he has reducedthe Emperorto a nonentity. I have grieved over this day and night. I have feared the Empire would be ruined.

You are a high Minister of State and my own relative. You must recall the difficulties of theGreat Founder’s early days and draw together the loyal and right-minded to destroy this evil faction and restore the prerogatives of the Throne. Such a deed would be indeed an extreme joy to the spirit; of my ancestors.

This decree, written in blood drawn from my own veins, is confided to a noble who is to be most careful not to fail in executinghis Emperor’s design.

Given in theera Established Tranquility, fourth year and the third month of Spring.

So ran the decree andDong Chengread it with streaming eyes. For him there was no sleep that night. Early in the morning he returned to his library to re-read it. No plan revealed itself to him. He laid the decree down on the table and sought in the depths of his mind for some scheme to destroyCao Cao, but could not decide upon any. And so he fell asleep, leaning over his table.

It happened that a certain official, Wang Zifu, with whom he was on terms of great intimacy, came to visit him and, as usual, walked into the house unannounced and went straight to the library. His host did not wake and Wang Zifu noticed, hardly hidden by his sleeve,the Emperor’s writing. Wondering what this might be he drew it out, read it, and put it in his own sleeve. Then he called out, loudly, “UncleCheng, are you not well? Why are you asleep at this time of day?”

Dong Chengstarted up and at once missed the decree. He was aghast; he almost fell to the ground.

“So you want to make away withCao Cao? I shall have to tell him,” said Wang Zifu.

“Then, brother, that is the end of theHans,” said his host.

“I was joking,” said Wang Zifu. “My forefathers also served theHansand ate of their bounty. Am I devoid of loyalty? I would help you, brother, as far as lies in my power.”

“It is well for the country that you think like this,” saidDong Cheng.

“But we ought to have a more private place than this to talk over such plans and pledge ourselves to sacrifice all in the cause ofHan.”

Dong Chengbegan to feel very satisfied. He produced a roll of white silk and wrote his own name at the top and signed it, and Wang Zifu followed suit.

Wang Zifu then proposed, “GeneralWu Zilanis one of my best friends, he ought to be allowed to come in.”

Dong Chengreplied, “Of all the officials of the courtChong JiandWu Shuoare my best friends. Certainly they would back me up.”

So the discussion proceeded. Presently a servant announced no other than these very two men.

“This is providential,” saidDong Chengand he told his friend to hide behind a screen.

The two guests were led into the library and after the exchange of the ordinary civilities and a cup of tea,Chong Jireferred to the incident at the hunt and the shooting of the stag. “Were you not angry at that?” said he.

Dong Chenganswered, “Though we be angry, what can we do?”

Wu Zilanstruck in, “I would slay this fellow, I swear, but I cannot get any one to back me up.”

“Though one should perish for one’s country one should not mind,” saidChong Ji.

At this moment Wang Zifu appeared from behind the screen, saying, “You two want to killCao Cao! I shall have to let him know this. AndUncleDongis my witness.”

“A loyal Minister does not mind death. If we are killed we will beHanghosts, which is better than being sycophants of a traitor.”

Dong Chengsaid, “We were just saying we wanted to see you two on this matter. Wang Zifu is only joking.”

Then he drew forth the decree and showed it to the two newcomers, who also wept as they read it. They were asked to add their names.

Wang Zifu said, “Wait here a few moments until I getWu Zilanto come.”

He left the room and very soon returned with his friend, who also wrote his name in the presence of all the others.

After this they went into one of the inner chambers to drink success to the new plot. While there a new visitor,Ma Teng,Grand AdministratorofLiang province, was announced.

“Say I am indisposed,” saidDong Cheng, “and cannot receive visitors.”

The doorkeeper took the message, whereatMa Tengangrily said, “Last night at theDonghua GateI saw him come out in robe and girdle. How can he pretend illness today? I am not come from mere idleness, why does he refuse to see me?”

The doorkeeper went in again and told his master what the visitor had said and that he was very angry. ThenDong Chengrose, excused himself saying he would soon return, and went to receiveMa Teng. After the visitor had saluted and they were both seated, he said, “I have just come from a farewell audience and wished to bid you adieu. Why did you want to put me off?”

“My poor body was taken suddenly ill; that is why I was not waiting to welcome you,” saidDong Cheng.

“You do not look as if you were ill; your face wears the very bloom of health,” saidMa Tengbluntly.

Dong Chengcould say no more and fell silent.

Ma Tengshook out his sleeves and rose to depart. He sighed deeply as he walked down the steps, saying to himself, “Not one of them is any good: there is no one to save the country.”

This speech sank deeply intoDong Cheng’s heart. He stopped his guest, saying, “Who is no good to save the country? Whom do you mean?”

“That incident at the hunt the other day, the shooting of the stag, filled my breast with anger. But if you, a near relative ofthe Emperor, can pass your time in wine and idle dalliance without a thought of doing away with rebellion, where can any one be found who will savethe dynasty?”

However,Dong Cheng’s doubts were not set at rest. Pretending great surprise he replied, “TheChancelloris of high rank and has the confidence of the court: why then does he utter such things?”

“So you find that wretchCao Caoa good man, eh?”

“Pray speak lower: there are eyes and ears very near us.”

“The sort of people who covet life and fear death are not those to discuss any great undertaking,” growledMa Teng. So saying he rose to go away.

By this timeDong Cheng’s doubts were set at rest: he felt thatMa Tengwas loyal and patriotic. So he said, “Do not be angry any more. I will show you something.”

Whereupon he invitedMa Tengto go into the room where the others were seated and then showed him the decree. AsMa Tengread it his hair stood on end, he ground his teeth, and bit his lips until blood began to flow.

“When you move, remember the whole force of my army is ready to help,” saidMa Teng.

Dong Chengintroduced him to the other conspirators and then the pledge was produced, andMa Tengwas told to sign his name. He did so, at the same time smearing the blood as a sign of the oath and saying, “I swear to die rather than betray this pledge.”

Pointing to the five he said, “We require ten in total for this business, and we can accomplish our design.”

“We cannot get many true and loyal men. One of the wrong sort will spoil all,” saidDong Cheng.

Ma Tengtold them to bring in the list of officials. He read on until he came to the name Liu, of the imperial clan, when clapping his hands he cried, “Why not consult him?”

“Whom?” cried they altogether.

Ma Tengvery slowly and deliberately spoke his name.

To a very trusty servant comes an Emperor’s decree,

And a scion of the ruling house can prove his loyalty.

If the reader turns to the next chapter, they will see whomMa Tengtalked about.
